nodes.context: Major rebel force in Myanmar's civil war, fought against the government.
Major rebel force representing Karen nationalists, seeking autonomy or independence.
Myanmar's armed forces, fighting ethnic armed organisations.
Ethnic insurgent group formed in response to refusal of federal system.
Insurgent group in Kokang, attacked by Tatmadaw in 2009.
Member of the Northern Alliance, attacked towns along China-Myanmar border.
Member of the Northern Alliance.
Insurgent group that attacked border posts in 2016 and 2017.
Armed wing of the KNU, attacked by Tatmadaw in 2006.
Armed wing of the National Unity Government, formed in 2021.
Civilian government-in-exile formed after 2021 coup, declared armed wing.
Signatory of Nationwide Ceasefire Agreement, joined National Unity Government.
Armed resistance group in Chin State, began operations in April 2021.
Armed group in Kayah State, clashed with Tatmadaw in 2021.
New armed wing of the Communist Party of Burma, created in late 2021.
Coalition of four insurgent groups: AA, KIA, MNDAA, TNLA.
Ethnic armed organization founded in 1960 to unify Chin-inhabited areas.
Separatist group fighting for Mizo independence.
Ethnic armed organization formed in 1988; signed ceasefire with Chin State government in 2012.
Separatist group founded in 1988 aiming for Kuki autonomy in Myanmar and India.
Separatist group from Northeast India operating from bases in Myanmar.
Separatist group from Northeast India operating from bases in Myanmar.
Separatist group from Northeast India operating from bases in Myanmar; headquarters in Taga attacked in 2019.
Armed wing of Kachin Independence Organisation; formed after 1962 coup; fought government forces.
Political organization of Kachin people; chairman N'Ban La as of 2018.
Pro-independence group founded in 1957 in Kayah State.
Armed wing of KNPP; fights for self-determination of Karenni people.
Leftist group; rival to KNPP; signed ceasefire in 1990s.
Leftist group; rival to KNPP; signed ceasefire in 1990s.
Civilian-led force formed after 2021 coup; opened new front in Kayah State.
Political organization of Karen people; signed ceasefire in 2015; resumed fight after 2021 coup.
Armed wing of KNU; fought for Karen independence.
Mon political organization formed in 1962 seeking self-determination.
Armed wing of NMSP; fighting since 1949; signed NCA in 2015.
Successor to Red Flag Communist Party in Rakhine; waged insurgency until 1990s.
Ethnic Rakhine insurgent group founded in 2009; largest in Rakhine State with ~7,000 fighters.
Ethnic Rakhine insurgent group fighting against government.
Rohingya insurgent group; claimed responsibility for attacks in 2016 and 2017.
Shan armed resistance movement formed in 1958; suffered internal split in 1960.
Splinter group from Noom Suk Harn formed in 1960 by young intellectuals.
Shan group operating in southern Shan State; merged into SSA in 1964.
Local army of Kokang Chinese; merged into SSA in 1964.
Merger of SSIA, SNUF, and Kokang Force in 1964; later fractured and collapsed in 1976.
Political wing formed in 1971 replacing Shan State War Council; later formed SSA-N.
Communist insurgent group active along China border; collapsed in 1989 after internal mutiny.
Pro-communist faction formed by SSPP after SSA collapse; signed ceasefire in 1989.
Splinter group from CPB led by Sai Leun; controls Mong La area (Special Region 4).
Shan insurgent group formed by Khun Sa in 1985, dominant in the 1990s.
Rival Shan organization formed by Shan nationalists who disagreed with Khun Sa's leadership of the MTA.
Shan insurgent group led by Yawd Serk, predecessor to SSA-S.
Shan insurgent group established in 1996 by Yawd Serk, one of the largest Shan groups.
Political wing of the SSA-S.
Ethnic armed group in Shan State, targeted by Tatmadaw in Operation Perseverance.
Shan insurgent group involved in fighting during Operation Perseverance.
Ethnic armed group in the region.
Ethnic armed group in Shan State, allied with MNDAA.
Ethnic armed group in the region.
Ethnic armed group in the region.
Communist insurgent group that controlled Kokang area from 1960s to 1989.
Kokang insurgent group founded in 1989, active in Kokang Self-Administered Zone.
Wa insurgent group founded in 1989 after split from CPB, governs Wa State.
Political wing of the UWSA.
Rohingya insurgent group with claimed links to private donors in Saudi Arabia and Pakistan.
Armed wing of the Karen National Union, fought alongside mercenary Dave Everett.
Major rebel force in post-independence period, backed out of ceasefire talks.
Coalition of insurgent groups allegedly linked to China.

edges.evidence_quote: The immediate post-independence period saw the rise of the Communist Party of Burma (CPB) and Karen National Union (KNU) in particular as major rebel forces.
edges.context: CPB fought against the Tatmadaw as a rebel force.

edges.evidence_quote: The immediate post-independence period saw the rise of the Communist Party of Burma (CPB) and Karen National Union (KNU) in particular as major rebel forces.
edges.context: KNU fought against the Tatmadaw as a rebel force.

edges.evidence_quote: Around this period, other ethnic minority groups began forming larger rebel factions, such as the Kachin Independence Army, in response to the new government's refusal to adopt a federal system.
edges.context: KIA formed as a rebel faction opposing the Tatmadaw-led government.

edges.evidence_quote: The Tatmadaw attacked Kokang in 2009, causing the Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army to lose control of the area
edges.context: Tatmadaw attacked MNDAA in Kokang in 2009.

edges.evidence_quote: In late November 2016, the Northern Alliance—which consists of four insurgent groups, the Arakan Army (AA), the Kachin Independence Army (KIA), the Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army (MNDAA) and the Ta'ang National Liberation Army (TNLA)— attacked towns and border posts along the China–Myanmar border
edges.context: AA as part of Northern Alliance attacked Tatmadaw positions.

edges.evidence_quote: In late November 2016, the Northern Alliance—which consists of four insurgent groups, the Arakan Army (AA), the Kachin Independence Army (KIA), the Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army (MNDAA) and the Ta'ang National Liberation Army (TNLA)— attacked towns and border posts along the China–Myanmar border
edges.context: TNLA as part of Northern Alliance attacked Tatmadaw positions.

edges.evidence_quote: On 9 October 2016, the Arakan Rohingya Salvation Army (ARSA) launched its first attack on Burmese border posts along the Bangladesh–Myanmar border, killing nine border officers. This prompted the Tatmadaw to begin massive 'clearance operations'
edges.context: ARSA attacked Tatmadaw border posts, leading to Tatmadaw clearance operations.

edges.evidence_quote: In 2006, the Tatmadaw launched a large-scale military offensive against the KNU's armed wing, the Karen National Liberation Army (KNLA).
edges.context: Tatmadaw launched offensive against KNLA in 2006.

edges.evidence_quote: It clashed with the Tatmadaw in the town of Muse on 23 May, killing at least 13 members of Myanmar's security forces.
edges.context: PDF clashed with Tatmadaw in Muse in May 2021.

edges.evidence_quote: The Chinland Defence Force began its armed resistance in Mindat and Hakha, Chin State, on 26 April 2021.
edges.context: Chinland Defence Force began armed resistance against Tatmadaw.

edges.evidence_quote: Members of the Karenni People's Defence Force (KPDF) in Kayah State also clashed with the Tatmadaw near the state capital of Loikaw.
edges.context: KPDF clashed with Tatmadaw in Kayah State.

edges.evidence_quote: The Communist Party of Burma rearmed itself and announced the creation of its new armed wing, the People's Liberation Army, in late 2021.
edges.context: People's Liberation Army is the armed wing of the Communist Party of Burma.

edges.evidence_quote: On 5 May 2021, the National Unity Government declared the formation of an armed wing, the People's Defence Force (PDF)
edges.context: PDF is the armed wing of the National Unity Government.

edges.evidence_quote: the Northern Alliance—which consists of four insurgent groups, the Arakan Army (AA), the Kachin Independence Army (KIA), the Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army (MNDAA) and the Ta'ang National Liberation Army (TNLA)
edges.context: AA and KIA are both members of the Northern Alliance, a coalition that attacks Tatmadaw.

edges.evidence_quote: the Northern Alliance—which consists of four insurgent groups, the Arakan Army (AA), the Kachin Independence Army (KIA), the Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army (MNDAA) and the Ta'ang National Liberation Army (TNLA)
edges.context: AA and MNDAA are both members of the Northern Alliance.

edges.evidence_quote: the Northern Alliance—which consists of four insurgent groups, the Arakan Army (AA), the Kachin Independence Army (KIA), the Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army (MNDAA) and the Ta'ang National Liberation Army (TNLA)
edges.context: AA and TNLA are both members of the Northern Alliance.

edges.evidence_quote: the Northern Alliance—which consists of four insurgent groups, the Arakan Army (AA), the Kachin Independence Army (KIA), the Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army (MNDAA) and the Ta'ang National Liberation Army (TNLA)
edges.context: KIA and MNDAA are both members of the Northern Alliance.

edges.evidence_quote: the Northern Alliance—which consists of four insurgent groups, the Arakan Army (AA), the Kachin Independence Army (KIA), the Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army (MNDAA) and the Ta'ang National Liberation Army (TNLA)
edges.context: KIA and TNLA are both members of the Northern Alliance.

edges.evidence_quote: the Northern Alliance—which consists of four insurgent groups, the Arakan Army (AA), the Kachin Independence Army (KIA), the Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army (MNDAA) and the Ta'ang National Liberation Army (TNLA)
edges.context: MNDAA and TNLA are both members of the Northern Alliance.

edges.evidence_quote: In 2006, the Tatmadaw launched a large-scale military offensive against the KNU's armed wing, the Karen National Liberation Army (KNLA).
edges.context: KNLA is the armed wing of the KNU.

edges.evidence_quote: Seven signatories of the Nationwide Ceasefire Agreement announced on 4 April 2021 that they had decided to join the National Unity Government, including the All Burma Student Democratic Front and the Karen National Union.
edges.context: All Burma Student Democratic Front joined the National Unity Government.

edges.evidence_quote: Seven signatories of the Nationwide Ceasefire Agreement announced on 4 April 2021 that they had decided to join the National Unity Government, including the All Burma Student Democratic Front and the Karen National Union.
edges.context: KNU joined the National Unity Government.

edges.evidence_quote: the Kachin Independence Army (KIA), under the Kachin Independence Organisation (KIO)
edges.context: KIA is the armed wing of KIO.

edges.evidence_quote: An accompanying armed wing, the Karenni Army, was established shortly after to fight for the self-determination of the Karenni people.
edges.context: Karenni Army is the armed wing of KNPP.

edges.evidence_quote: the Karen National Union (KNU) and its armed wing the Karen National Liberation Army (KNLA)
edges.context: KNLA is the armed wing of KNU.

edges.evidence_quote: The Mon National Liberation Army (MNLA) has been fighting government forces since 1949.
edges.context: MNLA is the armed wing of NMSP.

edges.evidence_quote: The SSPP later formed the pro-communist Shan State Army – North (SSA-N) to continue the insurgency.
edges.context: SSPP formed SSA-N as its armed wing.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivals to the KNPP include the leftist Kayan New Land Party (KNLP)
edges.context: KNLP is a rival to KNPP.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivals to the KNPP include the leftist Kayan New Land Party (KNLP) and Karenni National People's Liberation Front (KNPLF)
edges.context: KNPLF is a rival to KNPP.

edges.evidence_quote: It signed the Nationwide Ceasefire Agreement in 2015 and has had minor skirmishes with the Karen National Liberation Army (KNLA).
edges.context: MNLA had minor skirmishes with KNLA.

edges.evidence_quote: In northern Shan State, the SSA came into conflict with the Communist Party of Burma (CPB)
edges.context: SSA and CPB were in conflict.

edges.evidence_quote: After the CPB suffered an internal mutiny and collapsed in April 1989, the SSA-N signed a ceasefire agreement with the government
edges.context: SSA-N was pro-communist and formed after CPB's collapse, but no direct relationship stated.

edges.evidence_quote: A splinter group led by Sai Leun which broke away from the CPB at the same time established the National Democratic Alliance Army (NDAA)
edges.context: NDAA is a splinter group that broke away from CPB.

edges.evidence_quote: In 1960, the movement suffered an internal split between the 'rough and uneducated' Saw Yanda and a group of young intellectuals, and the latter subsequently formed the Shan State Independence Army (SSIA).
edges.context: SSIA split from Noom Suk Harn.

edges.evidence_quote: The SSIA, the SNUF, and the Kokang Force agreed to merge into the Shan State Army (SSA) in 1964.
edges.context: SSIA and SNUF merged to form SSA.

edges.evidence_quote: The SSIA, the SNUF, and the Kokang Force agreed to merge into the Shan State Army (SSA) in 1964.
edges.context: SSIA and Kokang Force merged to form SSA.

edges.evidence_quote: The SSIA, the SNUF, and the Kokang Force agreed to merge into the Shan State Army (SSA) in 1964.
edges.context: SNUF and Kokang Force merged to form SSA.

edges.evidence_quote: some Shan nationalists in the MTA disagreed with his leadership and formed a rival Shan organisation, the Shan State National Army (SSNA)
edges.context: SSNA split from MTA due to disagreement with Khun Sa's leadership.

edges.evidence_quote: Lieutenant General Yawd Serk of the Shan United Revolutionary Army (SURA) led 800 soldiers under his command to central Shan State and established the Shan State Army – South (SSA-S) on 26 January 1996
edges.context: SSA-S was established by Yawd Serk from SURA soldiers.

edges.evidence_quote: Its political wing is the Restoration Council of Shan State
edges.context: Restoration Council of Shan State is the political wing of SSA-S.

edges.evidence_quote: The MNDAA and Ta'ang National Liberation Army (TNLA) have carried out several joint attacks against the Tatmadaw in Shan State.
edges.context: MNDAA and TNLA conduct joint attacks against the Tatmadaw.

edges.evidence_quote: ethnic Wa cadres under the command of Bao Youxiang announced their split from the party and the founding of the United Wa State Party and United Wa State Army
edges.context: UWSA and UWSP were founded together as twin organizations.

edges.evidence_quote: Following the CPB's internal mutiny on 16 April 1989, ethnic Wa cadres under the command of Bao Youxiang announced their split from the party and the founding of the United Wa State Party and United Wa State Army
edges.context: UWSA split from CPB after internal mutiny.

edges.evidence_quote: From the 1960s to 1989, the Kokang area in northern Shan state was controlled by the Communist Party of Burma, and after the party's armed wing disbanded in 1989 it became a special region of Myanmar under the control of the Myanmar Nationalities Democratic Alliance Army (MNDAA)
edges.context: MNDAA succeeded CPB control in Kokang after CPB disbanded.

edges.evidence_quote: Dave Everett ... joined the Karen National Liberation Army as a mercenary. Everett fought alongside the KNLA
edges.context: KNLA is the armed wing of KNU.
